$description: The attention gradient maps emphasis levels to visual treatment across the design system. It answers: 'How prominent should this element be?' Every element exists somewhere on this gradient, from interactive (demands attention) to texture (the ghost of physicality). The gradient is a dhvani gradient — it mirrors the three meaning levels from Indian aesthetic theory: vācya (stated, interactive), lakṣyārtha (indicated, decorative), vyañjanā (suggested, ambient through texture). See aesthetic-theory.language.json for the theoretical framework. Three accent voices — gold (contemplative), ochre (communal), crimson (publication) — each express this gradient with voice-shaped opacity sets. Gold has 6 levels (contemplation needs the finest gradations from decorative to texture). Crimson has 5 (structure, not atmosphere — no texture level). Ochre has 4 (energetic, not contemplative — no highlight or texture). The asymmetry IS the design: each voice uses exactly the opacity levels its purpose demands. An orthogonal color axis — the motif system's five botanical voices (sacred, growth, devotion, warmth, crimson) — intersects with the gradient through opacity. Motif opacity follows the gradient's attention levels; motif hue follows the lotus's own devotional palette. The two dimensions are independent.

interactive.purpose: The seeker is actively engaging. This element demands and deserves full attention.

prominent.purpose: Important but not interactive. Draws the eye without requiring action.
prominent.note: Prominent is expressed through typography (bold weight, display size) and text color at full opacity — not through gold/crimson/ochre accent opacity. No accent-opacity CSS variable or utility class exists for this level. See three-voice-architecture.note-on-prominent.

decorative.purpose: Beautiful without calling. Background beauty that enriches without distracting.

treatment.size: large (decorative elements are often larger but visually lighter)

ambient.purpose: Peripheral awareness. Orientation without distraction — the seeker knows where they are without looking directly.

highlight.purpose: Guiding without grabbing. Showing the seeker where they are in the text.

subliminal.purpose: Felt, not seen. Registers as warmth or presence, not as a visible color.

treatment.motion: interaction speed (150ms) — fast because it shouldn't be noticed transitioning

texture.purpose: The ghost of physicality. Present only as a feeling — 'this doesn't feel like a screen.'
elements: paper texture overlay on reading surfaces

publication-accent.$description: The crimson attention gradient for publication surfaces. When content originates from a printed book (data-publication attribute), structural elements use crimson instead of gold. This creates two visual layers: the portal (gold) and the book (crimson). The reader unconsciously perceives both: 'I am using a portal to read a book.' Crimson never replaces gold for portal infrastructure (navigation, search, dwell, focus rings) — only for the book's own structural hierarchy.
publication-accent.etymology: The word 'crimson' comes from Sanskrit kṛmi-ja (कृमिज, 'produced by a worm') — the kermes insect dye. The word traveled from Sanskrit through Arabic (qirmiz) to Old Spanish to English. A word of Indian origin that became the standard for Western fine bookmaking. It is, literally, the bridge between the two traditions this design system serves.
publication-accent.cultural-lineage: This is the tradition of rubrication — from Latin rubrica (red earth) — chapter headings in red, practiced in Western manuscripts since the 4th century. SRF's choice of crimson for their most sacred text connects to this millennium-old bookmaking practice while simultaneously echoing Indian devotional red (kumkum, sindoor, the color of Shakti).

elements: chapter markers in table of contents sidebar
faint background warmth on publication reading surfaces — the binding glimpsed in peripheral vision

publication-accent.coexistence-with-gold: Gold remains for all portal-level elements even within a publication context. Focus rings: gold. Scroll progress: gold. Dwell mode highlight: gold. Links: gold. Chapter title: crimson. Section divider: crimson. Drop cap: crimson. The two colors partition the visual space by function, not by proximity.

communal-accent.$description: The ochre attention gradient for communal surfaces. When the gathering theme is active, the accent shifts from gold to ochre (#DC6A10). Ochre is energetic, warm, and welcoming — the joy of shared devotion. It does not need the contemplative gradient's fine distinctions.
communal-accent.etymology: From kāṣāya (काषाय) — the ochre of renunciation. The color of the monastic robe, traditionally dyed with tree bark (myrobalan, jackfruit, turmeric). Also gendā (गेंदा) — the Hindi marigold, the garland of welcome. And gairika (गैरिक, from giri = mountain) — the earth pigment used continuously from Bhimbetka cave paintings (30,000+ BCE) through today's temple tilaka. Gold and ochre are two poles of one kāṣāya spectrum: gold is the fresh robe's yellow, ochre is the sun-worn cloth's orange. Female monastics wear the yellow end; male monastics the deeper orange. The design system honors both.
communal-accent.cultural-lineage: The ochre tradition is the oldest continuous pigment in human history. Blombos Cave (South Africa, 75,000 BCE). Bhimbetka (India, 30,000+ BCE). Lascaux (France, 17,000 BCE). Aboriginal Australian ceremony (continuous to present). Ajanta frescoes. Temple tilaka. The renunciant's robe. The marigold garland offered at the threshold between the sacred and the human. Western ochre comes from Greek ōkhra (ὤχρα, pale yellow) — but the Indian tradition has its own older words: gairika, kāṣāya, geruā (गेरुआ, the specific ochre-orange of the monastic robe).
communal-accent.accent-color: ochre (#DC6A10, theme-adaptive via --color-gold remapping in gathering theme)
communal-accent.absent-levels: Ochre has no 'highlight' level (guiding through sustained reading is contemplative, not communal) and no 'texture' level (paper materiality belongs to the reading surface). Four levels is the correct count for a voice that says 'welcome and act,' not 'linger and contemplate.'

three-voice-architecture.$description: The attention gradient expresses through three accent voices. This is the final form — structurally complete.

gold.gradient: interactive → decorative → ambient → highlight → subliminal → texture

three-voice-architecture.note-on-prominent: The levels section above lists seven attention levels including 'prominent.' Prominent is expressed through typography (bold weight, display size), not through accent color opacity. It therefore does not appear in any voice's opacity gradient. Similarly, 'texture' is expressed through SVG fractalNoise at 3% opacity, not through a utility class — only gold has the texture token, because only the contemplative voice simulates physicality.
three-voice-architecture.hue-dependent-calibration: Warmer, more saturated hues (crimson #9B2335, terracotta #bb4f27) are perceptually heavier than lighter hues (gold #dcbd23). To achieve equivalent visual weight at the same attention level, they require lower opacity. Crimson ambient (0.25) is lower than gold ambient (0.3); crimson highlight (0.15) is lower than gold highlight (0.2). This principle applies wherever accent colors differ — it is not decorative tuning, it is perceptual calibration.
three-voice-architecture.voice-selection: Voice is determined by surface purpose, not content type. A Yogananda quote on a communal surface still renders in gold's sacred register — but the surrounding chrome uses ochre. See emotional-registers.language.json for the register × voice matrix.

motif-integration.$description: The motif system's botanical glyphs participate in the attention gradient through opacity, while maintaining their own five-voice devotional palette for hue. Motif opacity follows attention levels (decorative at 0.4, ambient at 0.3, etc.). Motif color follows the lotus's own palette. The two axes are independent: a growth-colored lotus at decorative opacity is olive-green at 40%, not gold at 40%.

sacred.bridge: Direct bridge to the contemplative voice. Default motif color.

growth.bridge: No direct voice mapping. The lotus's own vitality — the stem from which the bloom rises.

devotion.bridge: Nearly identical to contemplative text color (#1A2744). The lotus rendered in the ink of the page.

ochre.bridge: Unified with the communal accent. The lotus in the robe's own color — the ochre of renunciation. Previously 'warmth' (#dd6c26) and 'marigold' (#DC6A10), now recognized as one pigment.

crimson.bridge: The published book's structural color. Activates only under data-publication — structural motifs (divider, close) shift to crimson at decorative opacity. Breath motifs stay gold (reading rhythm is a portal feature, not a book structure).

motif-integration.palette-unity: These five voices are one devotional palette, not four-plus-one. Four were discovered archaeologically in the original yogananda.org lotus SVGs. The fifth was discovered in the AoY Kindle edition. The activation context differs (four always available, crimson only under data-publication) but the palette is continuous. Together they trace the Indian devotional spectrum: from the green of living nature through the gold of illumination to the crimson of sacred power.

secondary-text-hierarchy.$description: Text opacity levels for non-primary text. Follows the same gradient principle.

tertiary.use: Placeholder text, disabled labels, hint text

border-hierarchy.$description: Border visibility follows the attention gradient. Most boundaries in this design language are implied (whitespace, background shifts), not drawn. Two orthogonal axes: opacity (how much attention) and gradient (where attention gathers).

prominent.use: Section dividers, card borders on hover

subtle.use: Internal dividers within a card, table rows

none.use: Reading surfaces, clean content areas. Preferred.

gradient-axis.$description: Spatial attention dimension. The gradient flows along the container edge, expressing voice identity through color stops. Gold mediates — the bindu color at gradient center. The kāṣāya spectrum (gold → ochre) is a single pigment family at different stages of its journey; the gradient makes this continuity visible. Prāṇa made visible: pūraka (approach) as attention gathering at the container edge.
gradient-axis.technique: Dual background with background-clip. Inner layer shows surface color (padding-box); outer layer shows gradient (border-box). Works with border-radius. No pseudo-elements. Middle stop derived via color-mix(in oklch) for perceptually uniform darkening.

gold.use: Contemplative focus states. The reading lamp's glow.

ochre.use: Communal focus states. The kāṣāya spectrum — renunciant cloth warming through gold.

crimson.use: Publication containers. Rubrication tradition meeting the reading lamp.

triggers: elevated containers at interactive attention level
gradient-axis.forbidden: Never decorative-only. Never animated. Never on reading surface text containers — the teaching is the bindu; its container must not call attention to itself.
gradient-axis.automatic-voice: Gathering theme → ochre gradient. Publication context → crimson gradient. No manual class needed.
gradient-axis.accessibility: forced-colors: falls back to solid system border (ButtonText/Highlight). Print: falls back to 1px solid #999.
